sources.label: OpenAI: Best Practices for Prompt Engineering
Wei et al.: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning (2022)
Cheng et al.: Zero-shot Can Be Stronger than Few-shot (EMNLP 2025)
Meincke/Mollick: Decreasing Value of CoT (Wharton, Jun 2025)
Lakera: Ultimate Guide to Prompt Engineering 2026
Kim et al.: Scaling Agent Systems (Google/MIT, Dec 2025)
Thomas Wiegold: Prompt Engineering Best Practices 2026

claims.claim: The most successful LLM agent implementations use simple, composable patterns rather than complex frameworks. Anthropic observed this consistently across dozens of enterprise teams.
claims.soWhat: Simplicity beats complexity in production prompt engineering. Framework overhead often hides bugs.

claims.claim: Anthropic distinguishes 'workflows' (predefined code paths orchestrating LLMs) from 'agents' (LLMs dynamically directing their own processes). This distinction shapes prompt architecture.
claims.soWhat: Prompt design depends on whether the system is a workflow or an agent. Different patterns for each.

claims.claim: Anthropic recommends: start with the simplest solution. Optimizing single LLM calls with retrieval and in-context examples is usually enough. Only add agent complexity when needed.
claims.soWhat: Default to simple prompts + RAG before building agentic systems. Most tasks don't need agents.

claims.claim: OpenAI's best practice hierarchy: try zero-shot first, then few-shot, then fine-tune. Each step adds complexity and cost.
claims.soWhat: Zero-shot is the cheapest and fastest. Only escalate when quality drops below threshold.

claims.claim: For recent strong models (Qwen2.5, GPT-4+, Claude 3.5+), adding traditional CoT exemplars does NOT improve reasoning performance compared to Zero-Shot CoT. Their primary function is output format alignment.
claims.soWhat: CoT few-shot examples are increasingly unnecessary for strong models. Save tokens, use zero-shot CoT.

claims.claim: Even enhanced CoT exemplars (constructed from DeepSeek-R1, Qwen2.5-Max) fail to improve reasoning. Models tend to ignore exemplars and focus primarily on instructions.
claims.soWhat: The ICL+CoT paradigm may be fundamentally limited for math reasoning. Instruction quality > example quality.

claims.claim: Wharton research (Mollick et al., Jun 2025) reports decreasing value of Chain-of-Thought in prompting as models improve. 'Prompting Science Report 2'.
claims.soWhat: CoT's value is declining over time. Investment in CoT prompt templates may have diminishing returns.

claims.claim: Original CoT paper (Wei et al., 2022): chain-of-thought prompting — intermediate reasoning steps — significantly improves complex reasoning in LLMs. 11K+ citations.
claims.soWhat: Foundational finding, but now nuanced: works for older/weaker models, diminishing for strong models (see c05-c07).

claims.claim: Prompt scaffolding (wrapping user inputs in structured, guarded templates) limits model misbehavior against adversarial input. Production-essential for user-facing apps.
claims.soWhat: Prompt engineering is also a security practice, not just a quality practice.

claims.claim: Production advice (2026): keep prompts conversational, pin apps to specific model snapshots (e.g., gpt-5-2025-08-07), try zero-shot before few-shot.
claims.soWhat: Model router behavior changes between versions. Pinning prevents silent regressions.

claims.claim: Key prompt techniques taxonomy: zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, tree-of-thought, role/persona, prompt chaining, meta-prompting, reflection prompting.
claims.soWhat: 8 established techniques. Most production systems use only 2-3. Know all, apply selectively.

claims.claim: Prompt chaining decomposes tasks into sequential steps where each LLM call processes the previous output. Add programmatic checks between steps for quality gates.
claims.soWhat: This is what we do in AGENTS.md: Task Loop with intermediate checks. Validated by Anthropic.

claims.claim: Instructions at the beginning + clear delimiters (### or """) separating instruction from context significantly improve output quality vs. unstructured prompts.
claims.soWhat: Simple structural rules have outsized impact. Should be enforced in every system prompt template.

claims.claim: Prompt engineering is not just for developers. Some of the best prompt engineers are product managers, UX writers, or subject matter experts who know how to ask the right question.
claims.soWhat: For Ainary's BAFA consulting: prompt engineering skill is accessible to non-technical SME clients.

claims.claim: There is a fundamental tension: as models get stronger, explicit prompting techniques (CoT, few-shot) become less necessary. The 'prompt engineering' skill may converge toward clear instruction writing.
claims.soWhat: Long-term: prompt engineering as a discipline may decline. Investment should shift to system design + retrieval architecture.

starkBelegt: Zero-shot > Few-shot for strong models (EMNLP 2025, A2)
Prompt chaining with quality gates (Anthropic, A1)
unsicher: Model pinning advice from B3 source (single blogger)
Prompt engineering accessible to non-technical users (Lakera claim, no study)
nichtGefunden: Quantitative comparison of prompt techniques by task type (no meta-analysis found)
widersprueche: Original CoT paper (2022) claims significant reasoning improvement. 2025 papers (Cheng, Mollick) show this effect is diminishing for strong models. NOT a contradiction — temporal evolution.
followUp: How does prompt engineering differ for reasoning models (o1, DeepSeek-R1) vs. standard models?
What is the empirical cost difference between zero-shot and few-shot in production?
How do system prompts interact with user prompts in multi-turn conversations?
What prompt patterns are specific to tool-use / function-calling scenarios?
How does prompt scaffolding relate to prompt injection defense?
